topic: rude customer story — wanted a refund after a haircut

turns.content: let me tell you what happened to me today... I am still... I cannot believe it. a man sat in my chair, I cut his hair, he looked in the mirror, he said 'fine,' he paid, he left. 20 minutes later he comes back and says he wants his money back
Wait -- he said it was fine, paid, left, and then came BACK for a refund? That's bold. What was his reason? Did he suddenly decide he didn't like it, or did someone else say something about it?

exactly... he said his wife did not like it. his wife! I said to him, my friend... I cut YOUR hair, not your wife's hair. if your wife wants to choose the haircut she should come and sit in the chair next time. but he was serious... he wanted the money back

Ha! "If your wife wants to choose the haircut she should come and sit in the chair" -- that's a perfect response honestly. But yeah, that's not how services work. You can't eat a meal, leave the restaurant, and come back asking for a refund because your wife didn't like what you ordered.

Did you give him the refund, or did you hold your ground?

of course I did not give it... what am I supposed to do, glue the hair back on? there is a saying in Turkish... 'kesilmiş baş sarılmaz.' a cut head cannot be bandaged. what is done is done. but the man became angry... he raised his voice in my shop, in front of other customers

That's the worst part -- making a scene in front of other customers. That puts you in an awkward spot because even though you're 100% right, other customers watching might feel uncomfortable. How did you handle it when he started raising his voice?

I stayed calm... I have been doing this for a long time, you know. I said, 'kardeşim, brother, lower your voice. this is a place of respect. you approved the haircut, you paid, it is done. if there was a problem you should have said before you left.' and I offered him... I said next time I will give you 20% off. as a gesture

That's about as well as you could handle it. Staying calm, firm, respectful but not backing down -- and the 20% off next time is a smart move. You're not admitting fault but you're showing goodwill. Did he accept that?

no... he said he would never come back and he would leave bad reviews on Google. Google! I have been cutting hair since before Google existed. now a man can destroy your name with a phone. this is the world we live in... 'dünya yuvarlaktır' they say, the world is round, what goes around comes around. but still... it bothers me

I understand why it bothers you. A bad Google review from someone being unreasonable can feel really unfair. But here's the thing -- one bad review from a clearly unreasonable person won't hurt you if you have plenty of good ones. Do you have many Google reviews currently? If you have 50+ positive reviews, one angry one actually makes you look MORE credible -- people know some reviewers are just difficult.

If he does leave one, you can respond publicly. Something calm and professional like: "We're sorry you were unhappy. The haircut was approved before payment and we offered a discount on your next visit. We welcome all customers and strive to do our best." That makes YOU look good and him look petty.

hmm... this is good advice. I have maybe 30 reviews, mostly good. my nephew set up the page for me. but I never thought about responding... you are right, if I respond calmly it shows who is the reasonable one. you know... in the old days a man's word was enough. you shake hands, it is done. now everything is on the internet for everyone to see

The world has changed, but the principle is the same -- your reputation is built on how you treat people, and that comes through whether it's face-to-face or online. Thirty reviews with mostly good ratings is solid. You could also ask your regulars to leave reviews -- most happy customers never think to do it unless you ask. That builds a buffer against the occasional unreasonable person.

And for what it's worth, you handled the situation exactly right. Calm, respectful, didn't cave. That's the professional move.

yes... I will ask some of my regulars. the ones who have been coming for years, they would do it for me. you know what is funny though... in 30 years I have had maybe 3 customers like this. 3 out of thousands. but it is always the bad ones you remember... the good ones you take for granted

That's so true -- and it's human nature unfortunately. Three difficult customers out of thousands is an incredible track record. Most businesses would kill for that ratio. The problem is one bad experience takes up more mental space than a hundred good ones.

If it helps, try to think about the last customer who genuinely thanked you or came back specifically because they loved what you did. That's the real story of your shop -- not this one guy and his wife's opinion.

you are right... last week a man came in, he said his grandfather used to come to my shop before he passed. the grandson wanted the same haircut, the same experience. he sat in the chair and he said, 'my grandfather always said this was the best barber in Kadıköy.' this... this is what matters. not the refund man

That's a beautiful story -- a grandson seeking out the same barber his grandfather loved. That's legacy. That's what 30 years in the same chair earns you. No Google review, good or bad, comes close to that.

Hold onto that one when the refund guy keeps popping into your head. Alright, go take care of your next customer -- and maybe ask your regulars for those reviews this week while the motivation is fresh.

I will I will... okay, I feel better now. sometimes you just need to tell someone the story and let it out, you know. teşekkürler my friend. hopefully the next customer does not bring his wife to inspect afterwards, hah
Ha! Let's hope not. But if he does, at least you've got your response ready. Go take care of that next customer. Take care, and don't let one bad apple spoil your week.
